findings.problem: Once the June/Eli phrase overlap lands, the chapter keeps restating the same linguistic insight in narratorly terms about hinges, carpentry, and evidence. That repetition explains the uncanny after it has already registered on the page, so the closing movement loses some of its destabilizing force and starts reading like case-building.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or remove at least one of the explicit analytical beats at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:335, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:469, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:553. After the phrase echo at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:381, let Lena's suspicion advance through a fresh concrete action, bodily reaction, or changed behavior instead of another explanation of sentence mechanics. Keep one sharp articulation of why the shared stem alarms her, but do not restate the same clue in essay form at the close.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, after the echo at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:381, the remainder of the chapter contains no more than one explicit paragraph explaining the shared syntax, and the ending at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:547 lands on a new concrete consequence or investigative move rather than another restatement of "don't do the story where." Fail if the revised close still explains the hinge multiple times after June's line.

summary: Strong chapter with clean continuity, sustained Lena-centered interiority, and distinct Farah/June/Eli registers; the reverse-image hit, false-memory test, and June-echo beat all land, and I found no tense drift, sanitization, contraction-pattern, or continuity contradictions. Award fails by default, and prose fails because the chapter over-explains the linguistic clue after it has already registered, which slightly blunts the uncanny ending.
